title: Dog Urinating Sand or Grit: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Noticed sand-like particles in your dog's urine? Learn about crystalluria, when it becomes an emergency, and how to help your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is Grit or Sand in Dog Urine?

When you see tiny, sand-like particles in your dog’s urine, it is usually a sign of crystalluria. This condition occurs when minerals in the urine—such as struvite, calcium oxalate, or urate—become highly concentrated and solidify into microscopic crystals. If these crystals continue to accumulate, they can eventually fuse together to form bladder stones, also known as uroliths, which can irritate the bladder wall and lead to infection.

Is it an Emergency?

Finding grit in your dog's urine is considered a Medium Urgency situation. If your dog is still behaving normally, eating well, and able to produce a steady stream of urine, you should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours. However, if your dog is straining to urinate, crying out in pain, or unable to pass any urine at all, this is a life-threatening emergency. A urinary blockage can lead to kidney failure or a ruptured bladder within hours, so immediate intervention is required in those cases.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo of the urine and the gritty residue can be a vital tool for your veterinary team. Because crystals can sometimes settle or dissolve before a sample is professionally collected, a photo provides a "snapshot" of the problem in real-time. It helps the vet assess the color, quantity, and texture of the discharge, which allows them to better prioritize your dog's case and prepare the necessary diagnostic tools, such as an ultrasound or urinalysis, before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Can I treat urinary grit at home with more water?
faqs.answer: While increasing hydration is helpful, it is not a cure. You must see a vet to determine the crystal type, as some require specific prescription diets or medications to dissolve.

faqs.question: Does grit in the urine mean my dog has an infection?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily, but they often go hand-in-hand. Bacteria can change the pH of the urine, making it easier for crystals to form, while the crystals themselves can irritate the bladder and lead to secondary infections.

faqs.question: What diagnostic tests will the vet perform?
faqs.answer: Most vets will start with a urinalysis to look at the crystals under a microscope. They may also recommend X-rays or an ultrasound to check for larger bladder stones that aren't visible to the naked eye.
